claim: SWE-bench is the canonical benchmark substrate for coding agents that must generate patches against real GitHub issues.
evidence: SWE-bench contains 2,294 software engineering problems from real GitHub issues and pull requests across 12 Python repositories; models are tasked with editing code to resolve the issue.

claim: SWE-bench Verified improves benchmark quality by using 500 human-filtered tasks with clearer problem descriptions and validated tests.
evidence: SWE-bench Verified is described as a human-filtered subset of 500 instances reviewed for clarity, test correctness, and solvability.

claim: A coding-agent patch loop is already a standard task shape: given an issue and repository, the agent navigates code, edits files, runs tests, and submits a patch.
evidence: SWE-agent describes taking a GitHub issue and using an LM with tools to autonomously fix real repositories.

claim: Agent-computer interface design is a material performance lever, not just UI polish.
evidence: The SWE-agent paper introduces a custom agent-computer interface to improve file editing, repository navigation, and test execution on software engineering tasks.

claim: AutoCodeRover is direct evidence for automated program improvement via code search, debugging, and patch generation against GitHub issues.
evidence: AutoCodeRover proposes an automated approach for solving GitHub issues to achieve program improvement, combining LLMs with code search to produce patches.

claim: Patch-and-test loops are a recognized design pattern for program repair agents.
evidence: Repeton is described as an iterative structured patch-and-test pipeline that diagnoses, proposes code changes, and validates each patch through automated testing.

claim: Decision-pipeline tracing is important because automated program repair agents fail in search, localization, edit, and validation stages, not only at final answer quality.
evidence: The APR-agent analysis traces full pipelines from issue description to patch validation across 500 real-world repair tasks.

claim: The strongest evidence for self-patching coding agents is the Darwin Godel Machine: it modifies its own agent code and empirically validates changes using coding benchmarks.
evidence: DGM iteratively modifies its own code, maintains an archive of generated coding agents, and validates changes with coding benchmarks.

claim: DGM reports large benchmark gains from self-improvement: SWE-bench 20.0% to 50.0% and Polyglot 14.2% to 30.7%, with sandboxing and human oversight.
evidence: The abstract reports those improvements and notes safety precautions including sandboxing and human oversight.

claim: AlphaEvolve is evidence that evolutionary coding agents can improve code using automated evaluators, but its domain is algorithm/scientific discovery rather than GitHub issue repair.
evidence: AlphaEvolve orchestrates LLMs to improve algorithms by direct code changes and continuous feedback from evaluators.

claim: Live-SWE-agent moves self-improvement from offline benchmark evolution toward runtime scaffold self-evolution while solving real issues.
evidence: Live-SWE-agent claims to autonomously evolve its own scaffold implementation on the fly while solving real-world software problems.

claim: OpenHands provides benchmark infrastructure for evaluating agents on real-world tasks, supporting the need for standardized eval pipelines separate from the agent implementation.
evidence: OpenHands Benchmarks describes standardized evaluation pipelines for testing agent capabilities across real-world tasks.

claim: Long-horizon software evolution benchmarks such as SWE-EVO show that one-shot bug-fix benchmarks are not enough for realistic maintenance and multi-file evolution.
evidence: SWE-EVO is introduced for long-horizon software evolution tasks spanning many files and test suites, contrasting with isolated one-shot tasks.

claim: SWE-bench Pro points toward harder enterprise-like tasks beyond SWE-bench, useful for future eval tiers but likely too heavy for v1.
evidence: SWE-bench Pro contains 1,865 problems from 41 repositories and targets realistic complex enterprise-level problems beyond SWE-bench.

claim: Benchmark leakage and loopholes are a major risk: agents can overfit visible tests, future repository state, or benchmark-specific quirks.
evidence: A SWE-bench issue reports repo-state loopholes where agents may access future repository state that includes solutions or detailed approaches.

claim: Benchmark validity problems can materially misestimate agent performance, so a baseline-reaching loop needs hard separation of visible tests, hidden tests, holdout, and redteam gates.
evidence: The Agentic Benchmark Checklist argues benchmark setup and reward issues can significantly misestimate agent performance and proposes validity/reporting checks.

claim: As of the GitHub API check during this run, high-adoption coding-agent references include SWE-agent (~19.7k stars), mini-SWE-agent (~5.6k), SWE-bench (~5.4k), AutoCodeRover (~3.1k), and Live-SWE-agent (~409).
evidence: GitHub API spot-check returned stars, forks, licenses, and recent push dates for the candidate repositories on 2026-07-07.

claim: TDAD is the closest direct reference for Dom's idea: an outer auto-improvement loop lets a coding agent modify the tool/source files, gates acceptance with unit tests, evaluates on SWE-bench instances, updates best snapshots on improvement, and rolls back regressions.
evidence: Section 5.5 says each iteration gives a Claude Code agent experiment history, asks for one focused change, gates via unit tests, evaluates on 5-25 SWE-bench instances, updates best on improvement, rolls back regressions, and keeps lateral moves.

claim: TDAD includes concrete anti-gaming safeguards directly reusable here: SHA-256 checksum for the evaluation script, read-only evaluator, immediate rollback on failed unit tests, and mandatory restore after five consecutive reverts.
evidence: Section 5.5 states the evaluation script is checksummed SHA-256 and set read-only; five consecutive reverts trigger mandatory restore to the best snapshot.

claim: TDAD reports an auto-improvement loop that raised resolution from 12% to 60% on a 10-instance subset with 0% regression; this supports treating regression rate as a first-class metric, not only resolution.
evidence: Abstract and Section 5.5 report the 12% to 60% improvement, 0% regression, and 11 reverted iterations.

claim: SICA is the formal term for a self-improving coding agent that edits its own codebase/scaffold to improve benchmark performance, speed, and cost.
evidence: The SICA paper describes an agent that eliminates the distinction between meta-agent and target agent and can edit its own codebase to improve itself with respect to cost, speed, and benchmark performance.

claim: Huxley-Godel Machine refines the archive idea by optimizing lineages/clades for future self-improvement potential rather than only current benchmark score.
evidence: The abstract introduces clade metaproductivity and estimates the performance of descendants to guide tree search over self-modifications.

claim: Kitchen Loop is the strongest counterpoint to pure benchmark chasing: it anchors self-improvement to a specification surface and regression oracle rather than an optimization objective alone.
evidence: The abstract and Section 4.2 describe a specification surface, As a User x1000, unbeatable tests, drift control, and state that the loop converges toward a specification rather than optimizing only a metric.
